file_name: Khan et al. - 2022 - Evaluation of human melanoma and normal formalin paraffin-fixed samples using Raman and LIBS fused d.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning classification techniques, specifically Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), and K-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), to classify human melanoma and normal formalin paraffin-fixed tissue samples using fused Raman and LIBS spectral data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes a novel experimental study involving the fusion of Raman and LIBS spectroscopic data on human melanoma and normal FFPE tissue samples. It details specific methods (ELM, PLS-DA, kNN classification), experimental setups, and original results (100% accuracy for combined data), which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: Evaluation of human melanoma and normal formalin paraffin‑fixed samples using Raman and LIBS fused data
brief_summary: This study evaluated the classification of human melanoma and normal formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue samples using a novel fusion of Raman and laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) data. The researchers collected five melanoma and five normal FFPE tissue samples from clinical acquisitions to perform simultaneous elemental and molecular analysis. Spectral data were processed using three machine learning classifiers: Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), and K-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), alongside Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for visualization. The ELM model achieved the highest classification accuracy, reaching 100% for the fused LIBS-Raman data when using normalization preprocessing methods. The results demonstrate that combining Raman and LIBS spectroscopy significantly improves the identification and classification accuracy of melanoma compared to using either technique alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: Five melanoma cancer samples collected from patients of the metastasis stage (advanced stage) were used. The origin was lymph node. Confirmed by pathological slicing.
Five normal tissue samples collected from a patient with stage zero malignant melanoma. Origin was skin, proven by histology to be 95% normal sample.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of human melanoma and normal formalin paraffin-fixed (FFPE) tissue samples using fused Raman and LIBS spectral data. The study employed Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), and K-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) classifiers to distinguish between two discrete classes: melanoma cancerous tissues and normal tissues. The primary outcome metric was classification accuracy, with ELM achieving 100% accuracy on normalized fused data.

datasets_used.name: Human Melanoma and Normal FFPE Tissue Cohort
datasets_used.description: A small clinical cohort of formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue samples collected from two different patients with ethical permission from Chinese customs authority and sourced from Origene Technologies Inc. of America. The cohort consists of five melanoma cancer samples (metastasis stage, origin: lymph node) and five normal tissue samples (stage zero malignant melanoma patient, origin: skin, 95% normal by histology).

use.training: Spectral data from the samples were used to train classification models (ELM, kNN, PLS-DA). Specifically, 48 spectra were used to train the model for Raman data classification.
use.testing: Spectral data from the samples were used to test the classification models. Specifically, 32 spectra were used to test the model for Raman data classification.
use.other: The samples were also used for Principal Component Analysis (PCA) visualization and comparison of LIBS, Raman, and fused spectral data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for visualization of melanoma and normal data to extract critical information and remove overlapping information from Raman spectra.
Classification of normal and melanoma embedded tissues to achieve highest accuracy.
Classification of normal and melanoma embedded tissues to address massive complexity of the data set.
Classification of normal and melanoma embedded tissues by comparing unknown samples with a library of known samples.

statistical_methods_used.method: Normalization (by maximum and by total area)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Preprocessing technique applied to fused LIBS-Raman data to enhance classification accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It relied on classification accuracy metrics and visual inspection of PCA plots to compare the performance of different machine learning models and spectroscopic techniques.

Human Melanoma and Normal FFPE Tissue Co.dataset_description: 5 melanoma (metastasis stage, lymph node origin) and 5 normal (stage zero, skin origin) FFPE tissue samples. 48 spectra used for training, 32 for testing Raman data classification.

Raman Data Classification.notes: Classification performed using 48 spectra for training and 32 for testing. ELM achieved the highest accuracy among the three models for Raman data.

LIBS Data Classification.notes: ELM and PLS-DA achieved the highest accuracy (98%) for LIBS data. kNN achieved 93%.

Fused LIBS-Raman Data Classification.notes: Preprocessing methods included normalization by maximum and normalization by total area. ELM achieved 100% accuracy with both normalization methods. All samples were correctly categorized in the merged data case with optimal preprocessing.

Human Melanoma and Normal FFPE Tissue Co.notes: PCA was used for visualization. LIBS data showed clear distinction with slight overlap. Raman data showed considerable overlap. ELM generally outperformed kNN and PLS-DA, especially on fused data with normalization.

architecture_used.main_model: Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), and K-Nearest Neighbors (kNN)

ELM.details: Hidden layer node parameters are set spontaneously or arbitrarily and are not flexible. Only output weight calculation requires learning.

PLS-DA.details: Used to address the massive complexity of the dataset. Provides high classification power and automatic class prediction.

kNN.details: Classifies unknown samples by comparing them with a library of known samples. No a priori model is created. The only customizable parameter is k (number of closest neighbors).

conclusions: The fusion of Raman and LIBS spectroscopic data significantly improves the identification and classification accuracy of melanoma and normal FFPE tissue samples compared to using either technique alone.

The Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) classifier achieved the highest classification accuracy among the tested models (ELM, kNN, and PLS-DA), reaching 100% accuracy for fused LIBS-Raman data when using normalization preprocessing methods (by maximum and by total area).
For fused data, the ELM model achieved an average classification accuracy of 99.33%, outperforming kNN (90.33%) and PLS-DA (92%).
Preprocessing techniques, specifically normalization by maximum and normalization by total area, enhanced the classification accuracy of the fused data, particularly for the ELM model.
All samples in the fused data case were correctly categorized and classified, demonstrating the efficacy of the hybrid LIBS-Raman technique for melanoma diagnosis.

While ELM showed superior performance, the study acknowledges that kNN and PLS-DA models had varying performance levels, with kNN showing lower accuracy on normalized fused data and PLS-DA showing deficient results for normalized data in some contexts.
important_remarks: The study uses an extremely small sample size (n=10 total: 5 melanoma, 5 normal), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is derived from only two patients (one with metastatic melanoma, one with stage zero), introducing significant population bias and lack of diversity.
The normal control group consists of tissue from a melanoma patient (stage zero) that is only 95% normal by histology, rather than healthy controls.
The melanoma samples are exclusively from the metastasis stage (lymph node origin), limiting the applicability to early-stage or primary melanoma diagnosis.
The reported 100% classification accuracy for fused data with ELM is likely optimistic due to the small sample size and potential data leakage or overfitting risks inherent in such small cohorts.
No external validation was performed; the study relies solely on internal classification metrics from a single, small clinical acquisition.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the differences in classification accuracy between models.

The study acknowledges considerable spectral overlap in Raman data, suggesting that preprocessing (normalization) is critical for performance, which may not hold in larger, more heterogeneous datasets.
